title: NOC 72602 – Deck officers, water transport | GTR Immigration
description: In 2026, Deck Officers (NOC 72602) are the primary navigational commanders of Canada’s vast maritime corridors. As the country expands its Arctic shipping routes and invests in the "National Shipbuilding Strategy," these professionals are in high demand to operate and command ships, tugboats, and ferries. Operating at the TEER 2 level, the role has evolved to incorporate autonomous navigation support systems, electronic chart display and information systems (ECDIS), and advanced satellite communication. In 2026, the sector is prioritized for immigration, particularly in coastal provinces where a generational wave of retirements is creating a critical need for new officers.

classification.teer: FeatureDetailsBroad Occupational Category7 – Trades, transport and equipment operators and related occupationsTEER Category2 (Occupations usually requiring college, apprenticeship, or supervisory experience)Major Group72 – Technical trades and transportation officers and controllersSub-Major Group726 – Transportation officers and controllers

duties: Command & Operation: Commanding and operating ships or other self-propelled vessels to transport passengers and cargo on oceans and coastal/inland waters.
Navigation Planning: Planning and executing safe navigational passages using GPS, radar, and ECDIS, and determining geographical positions using charts.
Crew Supervision: Supervising and coordinating the activities of deck crews and overseeing the loading and unloading of cargo.
Vessel Safety: Monitoring vessel progress, weather, and sea conditions, and recording all activities in the ship’s log.
Pilotage Advice: (For Ship Pilots) Boarding vessels at berth or from pilot boats to advise captains on safe passage through hazardous or confined waterways.
employment_requirements: Education: Completion of secondary school and a Deck Officer Cadet program from an approved nautical institute is required.
Certification: A Deck Officer Certificate of Competency issued by Transport Canada is mandatory.
Experience: One to three years of experience as a member of a deck crew is typically required to begin officer training.
Specialized Licensing: Ship pilots require a specific Ship Pilot License and a Master Certificate of Competency.
Medical Fitness: Candidates must pass a Transport Canada marine medical examination, including strict eyesight and color vision requirements.
labour_market_demand: The 2026 outlook is Good, driven by fleet modernization and offshore energy projects:

British Columbia: High Opportunity. With nearly 2,000 workers, BC is the national hub for deck officers. It is classified as a "High Opportunity" occupation, with a median wage of $55.03/hr. Approximately 55% of the workforce is full-time, with high demand for tugboat captains and ferry masters in the Lower Mainland and Vancouver Island.

Ontario: Good prospects (2025-2027). In December 2025 and March 2026, Ontario issued targeted OINP invitations for NOC 72602 under the Employer Job Offer: Foreign Worker and International Student streams. This is especially true for regional hubs like the St. Lawrence Seaway and Eastern Ontario, where scores for invited candidates started at 53.

Newfoundland and Labrador: Very Good. The offshore oil industry and coastal ferry services are major employers. Roughly 750 people work in this occupation here, with over 75% employed in the water transportation sector.
National Trend: While the work can be seasonal (41-47% work part of the year depending on the province), deck officers work an average of 39-40 weeks per year, providing a stable annual income.
Why Work in Canada as a Deck Officer?
Lead Maritime Modernization: Gain experience with 2026-grade technologies, including dynamic positioning (DP) systems and integrated bridge systems on new Coast Guard and commercial vessels.
Diverse Sector Opportunities: Work for the Canadian Coast Guard (27% of the sector), international shipping lines, tug and barge companies, or provincial ferry authorities.
Elite Compensation: As a TEER 2 professional, your earning potential is significant. High-tier ship pilots and master mariners in 2026 earn upwards of $180,000 annually.
Immigration Priority: In early 2026, this NOC has been a consistent highlight in Ontario OINP regional draws and is a preferred occupation in the Atlantic Immigration Program (AIP).
pathways: OINP Employer Job Offer: In March 2026, OINP issued invitations to candidates in NOC 72602 with a score of 53+ under the Regional Economic Development (REDI) pilot. This is a high-success route for those with job offers in Eastern or Northern Ontario.

Atlantic Immigration Program (AIP): A primary route for deck officers. AIP allows employers in Atlantic Canada to hire international talent for "High-Skilled" (TEER 2) roles with faster processing and lower language requirements.

Express Entry (Federal Skilled Worker): As a TEER 2 occupation, NOC 72602 is eligible for all general Express Entry draws. Note: As of February 2026, the specific "Transport" category draws do not currently include this NOC (focusing on pilots/mechanics), making PNP and AIP the preferred routes.
2026 TR to PR Pathway: A confirmed initiative targeting up to 33,000 temporary residents. Deck officers currently working in Canada are classified as "High-Priority Transport Infrastructure Workers."
